review:
Counter-Strike is where it all started. Simple, raw, and purely skill-based. No fancy mechanics, no distractions — just aim, movement, map knowledge, and teamwork.

Every round feels intense, and even small plays can make a huge difference. The game rewards practice and experience more than almost any other shooter.

Even after all these years, Counter-Strike is still fun and incredibly competitive. A true classic that defined FPS games and will always have a special place in gaming history.

A classic. I'm in the younger generation of players for this game, my dad introduced me to it when I was five. Almost 20 years later and I've moved out and am studying at university about 150km away from my parents. I get to hang out with my dad through this game, which I think is pretty cool :)

Additionally, the game is good fun all around with it's many different gamemode servers. You have the classics like surf, gungame, zombie, kreedz and deathrun. I recently revisited the game and aside from the nostalgia blast of all the old maps, found out theres a warcraft gamemode too!! There's a corner for basically everyone in this game.

Counter-Strike 1.6 is more than just a game - it’s a memory, a feeling, a whole era. About 15 years ago, my friends and I used to gather in small internet cafes after school, packing into tight rooms full of noisy PCs, shouting across the tables, and connecting through a local network just to battle it out in cs. No fancy matchmaking, no skins, no battle passes - just pure competition and endless fun

What makes cs truly special is that time hasn’t killed it. Even after all these years, it remains a classic that refuses to fade away. The gameplay is simple, fast, and incredibly skill-based. Every round still feels tense, every clutch still makes your heart race and every headshot still feels earned. Modern shooters may look better, but very few feel this honest and raw

Even today, the community is alive. There are tons of servers running modes like war3ft, deathrun and Surf - and they are still insanely fun. Jumping into these servers instantly brings back that old-school vibe and a wave of nostalgia that modern games just can’t replicate. cs isn’t just remembered - it’s still played, still loved and still awesome. A true timeless legend

The Half-Life mod that changed the online FPS world forever.

It's actually a light tactical shooter where one team plays the terrorists with the objective to plant the bomb, and the other team the counter-terrorists with the objective to defuse the bomb, both teams having a plethora of weapons and gadgets on dispose.

A simple concept, but one that stood the test of time, as we can see in the refreshed versions of this game.

The OG C-S has a certain feel to it that is unique and not trully replicated again (although "Condition Zero" is very similar to it), I like how some weapons feel, the movement and everything.

It is still played and populated today, so you're free to check out some community servers, or make your own one and invite some friends over.

A classic "evergreen" title that also marked the gradual shift towards more realism-oriented shooters, opposed to purely fantasy/Sci-Fi arcade ones (which I also enjoy, but they're a different experience).
First game that my parents recommend me about FPS genre and nowdays, this masterpiece still keeps going and revolutionized games for decades. Thank you CS<3

Absolutely Not Recommended

I genuinely cannot stand Counter-Strike (2000). I don’t care how “legendary” people say it is - being old and influential doesn’t magically make it good.

This game feels like a museum exhibit that someone forgot to retire. The movement is clunky, the gunplay feels like it was designed to punish you personally, and the recoil system is less “skill-based” and more “memorize this invisible nonsense or suffer.” It’s not intuitive. It’s not welcoming. It’s not fun.

The graphics look like they were assembled out of leftover cardboard, the animations are stiff, and the sound design is so flat it might as well be placeholder audio that accidentally shipped. Every match feels less like tactical depth and more like getting instantly deleted by someone who has been practicing the same two maps for the last 20 years.

There’s no meaningful onboarding, no quality-of-life features, and absolutely no forgiveness for new players. You don’t “learn” the game - you endure it. And if you’re not already part of the nostalgia club, you’re just cannon fodder for veterans who treat public lobbies like their personal highlight reel.

People call it hardcore. I call it outdated and painfully overrated.

Respect its historical impact if you want.
Playing it in 2026? Miserable.

CS 1.6: My Childhood. My Roots. 🕊️
Most kids grew up with toys; I grew up with a laptop. My mom gave me my first one when I was only 1 year old. By the time I was 4 or 5, I was already actively playing CS 1.6.
I wasn’t chasing headshots or trying to be a pro. I was just playing. I lived in those maps—the echoes of de_dust2, the tension of cs_mansion, and the classic radio commands. It was the simplest, purest part of my childhood.
I’m 16 now and finally here on Steam, but 1.6 will always be where it all started.
Thank you, 1.6, for the memories. A true legend never dies. ❤️

This game is publicly "dead," as the kids say. However, if you play with large groups of friends and/or are willing to find the right online communities, it's a very fun time. Compared to TF2, which I'm sure is what people are checking reviews to see, it's functionally way jankier, has an infinitely higher skill curve, and is far less customisable. It also has a community of approximately 20 people, 4 gnats, and a ball of lint. Despite this, it has a great variety of potential playstyles and game modes (both official and fan-made) going for it despite what may appear at first glance to be barely anything. A fun game for people who want to find a new niche community to bond over a dorky interest with. I'd say the gameplay itself is more serious and competitive than a TF2 match with friends while the community is more goofy and closely connected than TF2's.

Team Fortress Classic is a pivotal piece of gaming history whose influence far exceeds its modern playability. As the foundation for the class-based shooter genre, its DNA is visible everywhere today. Yet, returning to it now reveals a game that is equal parts charming, raw, and frustratingly dated.

Strengths: Pure, Archetypal Design
Long before "hero shooters," TFC established the iconic class roster—Scout, Soldier, Heavy, Medic, and others—that remains a blueprint. Each role is distinct and mechanically pure. The exhilaration of the Scout’s speed, the skill-ceiling of rocket jumping, and the mind-games of the Spy still shine. Its simplicity is a virtue: objectives are clear, combat is swift, and there’s a refreshing lack of modern clutter like unlocks or microtransactions. It’s pure, skill-based gameplay.

Weaknesses: Dated and Unforgiving
However, that simplicity feels archaic today. Movement is fast but lacks polish, weapon feedback is weak, and the GoldSrc engine shows its age in blocky visuals and sparse audio. Map design, while iconic, often relies on choke points and narrow corridors, leading to defensive stalemates—especially against stacked Engineer sentries. Balance feels harsh and inconsistent by modern standards, with certain classes (Heavy, Sniper) easily tipping matches depending on context. The game demands coordination, yet public play often devolves into chaotic or stagnant bouts.

Verdict
Team Fortress Classic is an essential historical artifact that genre fans should experience. Its core class design is brilliant and influential. However, as a modern multiplayer experience, it’s held back by dated mechanics, technical limitations, and uneven pacing. The score of 6/10 reflects this divide: immense respect for its legacy, but a clear-eyed acknowledgment that it’s more a museum piece than a living game for most players.

X2: The Threat, developed and published by Egosoft, represents a significant evolution of the sandbox space simulation formula established in X: Beyond the Frontier. Expanding both the narrative scope and the systemic complexity of its predecessor, X2 invites players into a vast, interconnected universe where trading, combat, exploration, and empire-building coexist within a living economic framework. Rather than presenting a tightly scripted experience, the game embraces player freedom, offering a universe that operates independently of the player’s immediate actions and rewarding long-term planning over short bursts of action.

Set once again in the X-Universe, the story follows Julian Gardna, whose personal journey becomes entangled with the emergence of a mysterious and aggressive alien race known as the Khaak. The narrative provides structure and occasional urgency, especially during key missions that advance the plot. However, like many sandbox simulations, the storyline is optional in spirit if not in design. Players can follow the narrative threads at their own pace or temporarily ignore them in favor of building wealth, exploring uncharted sectors, or assembling fleets. The game’s real strength lies not in its cinematic storytelling but in its capacity to support player-defined goals within a sprawling galactic economy.

The economic system is the beating heart of X2: The Threat. Every station, factory, and ship participates in a dynamic supply-and-demand network. Prices fluctuate based on production levels and consumption rates, creating opportunities for savvy traders to buy low and sell high across dozens of sectors. Early in the game, players often start with modest ships and limited cargo capacity, forcing careful planning of trade routes. As profits accumulate, options expand—larger freighters can be purchased, automated trading ships can be assigned to generate passive income, and eventually entire industrial complexes can be constructed. This gradual progression from small-scale merchant to interstellar magnate is one of the game’s most satisfying arcs.

Combat in X2 adds another layer of depth, though it is rarely the sole focus. The universe contains pirates, hostile factions, and the looming Khaak threat, each capable of disrupting trade routes or attacking vulnerable assets. Players can outfit ships with a variety of weapons, shields, and upgrades, customizing their vessels for defense or offense. While dogfighting can feel less refined than dedicated space combat simulators, strategic fleet management compensates for this. Owning multiple ships and coordinating their deployment across sectors introduces a grander tactical dimension. Large-scale engagements involving capital ships and carriers create moments of spectacle, particularly when defending valuable infrastructure from enemy incursions.

The scale of the universe is impressive, comprising well over a hundred sectors connected by jump gates. Each region has its own economic profile, faction presence, and environmental character. Some sectors teem with trade opportunities, while others are dangerous frontiers patrolled by aggressive forces. Exploration feels purposeful, as uncovering new sectors often reveals lucrative trade possibilities or strategic locations for station construction. The sense of space is reinforced by ambient sound design and the relative isolation of deep-sector travel, contributing to immersion even when gameplay unfolds at a deliberate pace.

Despite its ambition, X2: The Threat demands patience. The interface, while improved over its predecessor, remains dense and occasionally unintuitive. Navigating menus, configuring ships, and managing multiple assets can feel overwhelming for newcomers. The early hours are especially slow, as players work within tight financial constraints and limited ship capabilities. Yet this slow start is intentional, mirroring the climb from obscurity to influence. The game rewards persistence; mastering its systems transforms complexity into empowerment.

Visually, X2 offered notable improvements at the time of release, with enhanced ship models, atmospheric nebulae, and more detailed station designs. While modern standards may reveal the age of its graphics engine, the aesthetic still conveys a sense of scale and cosmic vastness. Ship interiors and menus are functional rather than flashy, reinforcing the simulation-driven focus. The soundtrack and ambient audio contribute to the mood without dominating the experience, allowing players to settle into the rhythms of space commerce and exploration.

Ultimately, X2: The Threat stands as a defining chapter in Egosoft’s space simulation lineage. It successfully broadens the sandbox concept by layering narrative, economic depth, and fleet management into a cohesive universe. It may not offer immediate gratification or streamlined accessibility, but its systemic richness provides enduring appeal. For players who relish autonomy, complex economic systems, and the gradual construction of a personal interstellar empire, X2 delivers a rewarding and expansive journey through the stars.

X: Beyond the Frontier, developed and published by Egosoft, marks the beginning of a long-running space simulation series that would go on to define a particular niche of sandbox-driven, economy-focused gameplay. Released at the end of the 1990s, it arrived at a time when space simulators were either heavily combat-oriented or fading in popularity. Instead of following the fast-paced dogfighting model of many contemporaries, X: Beyond the Frontier chose a slower, more methodical path, emphasizing trading, economic systems, and open-ended exploration. The result is a game that feels less like a traditional action title and more like a living simulation of a complex interstellar society.

The narrative premise is straightforward but effective. Players assume the role of Kyle Brennan, a test pilot who becomes stranded in an unfamiliar region of space after an experimental jump drive malfunctions. Cut off from Earth and without immediate means of return, he must survive and adapt within a network of alien-controlled star systems. This setup provides context, but the story never aggressively dictates the pace of play. Instead, it functions as a loose thread woven into a much larger sandbox. The player is free to pursue narrative objectives or ignore them entirely, carving out their own path through commerce, exploration, and gradual expansion.

The true heart of X: Beyond the Frontier lies in its economic model. Each star system contains stations and factories that produce and consume specific resources, creating a dynamic supply-and-demand network. Players can buy goods where they are abundant and cheap, then transport them to sectors where they are scarce and valuable. Over time, profits allow for ship upgrades, better equipment, and eventually the construction of player-owned factories that generate income autonomously. This progression transforms the experience from a lone pilot scraping together credits into a budding industrial magnate influencing entire trade routes. The economic layer is surprisingly deep for its era, demanding attention to market fluctuations and logistical efficiency.

Combat exists but is not the primary focus. Encounters with pirates or hostile factions can occur, and upgrading shields and weapons becomes necessary for survival. However, dogfighting lacks the arcade immediacy found in more action-oriented space games. Battles tend to feel deliberate rather than frantic, reinforcing the broader theme that preparation and investment are more important than reflexes alone. In many cases, avoiding conflict or building enough economic power to outpace threats proves more rewarding than direct confrontation. This design choice underscores the simulation-driven philosophy at the game’s core.

Exploration plays a significant role in shaping the player’s journey. The universe is composed of numerous interconnected sectors linked by jump gates, each with distinct layouts and economic profiles. Discovering new systems often means uncovering fresh trading opportunities or encountering different factions with their own technologies and ships. The scale of the universe fosters a sense of isolation and curiosity, enhanced by ambient sound design and the quiet vastness of space. Unlike tightly scripted games, X: Beyond the Frontier allows long stretches of peaceful travel punctuated by moments of tension, mirroring the rhythms of real commerce and exploration.

Technically and visually, the game reflects its late-1990s origins. Ship models and station designs are simple by modern standards, and the interface can feel clunky to newcomers. Navigating menus, managing cargo, and issuing commands require patience, particularly for players accustomed to streamlined contemporary design. However, beneath the dated presentation lies a robust simulation that rewards dedication. The learning curve is steep, and early hours can feel slow or even confusing. Yet for those willing to invest time, the gradual mastery of systems and accumulation of wealth becomes deeply satisfying.

One of the game’s defining qualities is its emphasis on player autonomy. There are no rigid mission structures forcing progression, no scripted sequences dictating every action. Instead, X: Beyond the Frontier trusts players to set their own goals. Some may focus entirely on trading, others on exploration or incremental military strength. The emergent stories that arise—from narrowly escaping pirate ambushes to watching a newly built factory begin generating profit—often feel more personal and memorable than scripted events.

In retrospect, X: Beyond the Frontier stands as a foundational entry in the space simulation genre. It established the economic and sandbox framework that later entries in the X series would refine and expand upon. While its age is evident in both visuals and interface design, its ambition and systemic depth remain noteworthy. For players who appreciate open-ended progression, complex economic networks, and the quiet satisfaction of building an interstellar enterprise from humble beginnings, it offers a uniquely immersive experience. It is not a game of instant gratification, but rather one of patience, planning, and steady growth in a vast and indifferent universe.

X: Tension, developed and published by Egosoft, is a space simulation sandbox that significantly expands upon the framework established in its predecessor, X: Beyond the Frontier. Rather than functioning as a traditional sequel with a tightly scripted campaign, it acts as a large-scale evolution of the original concept, broadening the universe, increasing player freedom, and deepening economic and fleet management systems. The result is a title that prioritizes autonomy and systemic complexity over narrative guidance.

At its core, X: Tension places players in a vast interconnected galaxy composed of dozens of sectors, each governed by dynamic economic rules and populated by diverse factions. Unlike story-driven space games that funnel players toward fixed objectives, this title encourages self-directed progression. You are free to pursue commerce, piracy, bounty hunting, or large-scale economic expansion. The galaxy operates in real time, with goods being produced, transported, and consumed regardless of player involvement. This creates an ecosystem where supply and demand fluctuate organically, rewarding attentive traders who study market trends and identify profitable routes.

One of the most significant improvements over its predecessor is expanded ship accessibility. Players are no longer confined to a single vessel; instead, they can pilot a wide range of ships, from agile fighters to heavy transporters and freighters. Over time, this freedom extends into fleet management and station ownership. Establishing factories and production chains transforms the experience from simple trading into empire building. Constructing and defending your own industrial network adds strategic depth, as economic success must be balanced against threats from hostile factions and pirate activity.

Combat remains an integral but optional component of the experience. Dogfights occur in open 3D space, requiring careful maneuvering and energy management. Hostile factions such as the Xenon present ongoing dangers, particularly in border sectors. However, unlike pure action space shooters, combat here serves as part of a broader sandbox system. You may choose to avoid conflict entirely and focus on commerce, or you may invest heavily in military hardware to dominate contested regions. The game rarely forces a singular approach.

The interface and mechanics reflect the era of its release. Menus are dense and sometimes unintuitive, requiring patience to master. Yet beneath this complexity lies an intricate simulation system that rewards long-term investment. Learning how to manage automated traders, optimize supply chains, and monitor sector maps becomes part of the strategic appeal. The early hours can feel slow and methodical, but as wealth accumulates and fleets expand, the scale of operations grows dramatically.

Visually, X: Tension is unmistakably a product of the early 2000s, with modest ship models and relatively simple environmental textures. However, the sense of scale remains impressive. Vast starfields, distant nebulae, and the quiet hum of space travel create a contemplative atmosphere. Long trade routes and jump gate transitions reinforce the feeling of traversing a living galaxy rather than isolated mission zones. The soundtrack complements this tone with ambient compositions that emphasize exploration and solitude.

Narrative elements are minimal compared to the first game. While there is a loose framework connecting events in the X universe, the emphasis shifts strongly toward sandbox autonomy. Story moments exist primarily to provide context rather than dictate direction. This design philosophy makes the game particularly appealing to players who prefer emergent gameplay over scripted sequences.

Ultimately, X: Tension stands as a foundational entry in the X series that helped define Egosoft’s long-running formula. Its expansive economy, player-driven progression, and open-ended structure laid the groundwork for future installments. Though dated in presentation and occasionally opaque in interface design, it remains a compelling sandbox for players who appreciate deep simulation systems and the freedom to shape their own path through a living galaxy.

That's the first X game after X Beyond the frontier where I spent more than just a few hours. Many people say it's nothing like X3 or X4, and I think that's actually the great thing about it. It is simpler. To the point is actually playable with a controller and that's exactly the way I play it. Moreover, I play it on my Android phone (OnePlus 13) via Proton and box64 and get 20-30 fps just fine. Perfect couch gaming to relax. So far the story is engaging enough, and the gameplay is smooth and enjoyable.
If you're looking for a Freelancer-like experience (and not for a spreadsheet simulator your typical X game is), give it a try.

I don't recommend this game. I absolutely loved the 110 hours I spent there. The reason for a lack of recommendation is that the game is really beautiful in the way its mechanics work, to me. People complain abt how it is clunky or obtuse, but I don't agree. If you spend time and care to learn, it all works very well. But the problem is, when you haven't learned yet, it feels really really unapproachable. An example: early on I would interprete a lot of things as bugs. I had a Taranis (the mission one) that didn't have jump fuel and couldn't flyt and it hovered there forever despite me hiring an engineer and I gave up and sold it. Way later, 30 hours in, I knew that a ship like this with a 5 star engineer can be resuscitated, and captured a warden destroyer taranis to "catch up" with the campaign gift I lost xD 
I was also completely surprized at HOW MUCH happens at the same time. Raids come to my stations in far off systems, economy is churning, ships do seem to exist regardless of where I am (which I learned the hard way by having my Ship Tech Fab's CV destroyed in raids several times, first protected with that Taranis, then it wasn't enough and I bought 20 fighters and shoveled them there too). This is very cool! But the GAME. DOESN'T. TEACH. ANY. OF THAT. 
You can't mass join/leave squad from the menu. WHich is why I thought broadcast feature is useless. But you CAN assign superiors by mass broadcasts. I learned this on hour 80 of playing, after spending ridiculous amounts of time assigning ships one by one. When you craft SETA the game tells you pretty much nothing abt its operation, I had to google it to realize a little SETA icon was added (and it's activated with a hotkey anyway, or from a menu hidden way at the bottom). No tutorial, no "oh wow we crafted SETA, let's try it out!" from Yisha, NOTHING. This is just 2 examples of many MANY MANY cases when the game behaves as if you know the game. The coregameplay loop is: learn a process - encounter an unexplainable and out-of-left-field-obstacle (the most famous example would be fusion reactors that had some players wonder if it's bugged when it's a seemingly intentional obstacle to overcome) - think it's a bug - try problem solving around it - fail - google - it wasn't a bug after all! 
This is at times maddening. But after you do learn some of the game's hidden interconnected systems and how it works, you start getting it, you start having fun problem solving around a shipyard that stopped making ships due to a resource shortage and other issues like this. Some problems however will remain unexplainable at all (even after 110 hours I can't fix one such shipyard, it has an energy cell shortage but it doesn't buy them, at all, anywhere. All 3 construction cradles are stuck mid-construction and that shipyard is completely unaccessable for me, and has been for the last 80 hours of gameplay. Forced me to explore the map more and discover Home of Light, though! xD)

It's a burning pile of garbage.

Maybe the devs tried to recapture the experience of playing X: Beyond the frontier, thier first game in the series. Just like in X:BtF, you control a single ship, but while in XBtF you were in control of the Xperimental Shuttle, in X:R, you're in control of the Albion Skunk (aptly named because it stinks).
In X:BtF, while the start was slow and tideous, by the later stage of the game, your ship turned into the most outrageously overpowered thing in the known universe. In this, by the end of the game, you ship is slighly less ♥♥♥♥ than at the start, but you still can't do much. The whole "sacrifice your shields for some extra speed" is just a bad concept, in fact, they removed it and gave boosting its own bar in X4, the sequel game which is just so much better.

The X Universe games have a motto : Fight, Trade, Build, Think, so lets see how this game fits in those categories.

Fight : You weapons are weak, they barely do any kind of damage, they have a ridiculously short range for most of them, they overheat in the blink of an eye, and the whole swapping back and forth can be frustrating in the heat of the battle. Not only that, but NPC ships of the similar size/function are generally better. The Skunk with all its weapons, shields and upgraded to the best ones you can get, will still get wrecked by a bunch of pirates because your time to kill is abysmal, you can't eliminate threats fast enough. Even using missiles has a drawback if an enemy is shooting at you when you fire it, because the missile will get hit and explode in your face.

Trade : This one is bad. You'll have more fun day trading in real life than doing whatever this. You open the trade menu, you find a good deal, you select the freighter that you want to use to make that deal and send it to get it, rince and repeat (except that you have limited trade commands unless you upgrade the software on your ship. I know it's ~900 years in the future, but calling subordinates and maintaining and excel sheet is too hard for the cutting edge on board AI of the Skunk. Oh, almost forgot, to get the damned up to date trade deals, you have to have an informant on board the station, which you get by doing the "smalltalk" mini game, which consists of lingering near the NPCs on stations, waiting for someone to say something, accosting them and playing a timing minigame. THE DIALOGUES CAN'T BE SKIPPED.

Build : You get a construction vessel, you pick a preapproved plot for the station you want to build, you supply the materials (hard because of the plot turning the main suppliers against you) and waiting until it's done.

Think : I still think about all the different things I could've done with the money I spent buying this game.

Now, some rapid fire complaints : 

-Weapons do no damge
-Upgraded weapons do no damage and overheat faster
-Some enemies damage bypasses shields (looking at you, Xenon)
-Some of your projectiles will clip through enemies. I don't know what causes this.
-The highways are idiotic. If there's an M-Sized ship in front of you, have fun getting ejected over and over again. I've tried bypassing them by going to the sides, or above/below them, but no, you bump into them and you get ejected. In fact, the whole highway "mini game" of having to dodge traffic is badly implemented, you see a green or red trail behind ships in the highway. If it's red, you will collide with the ship, if it's green, you're safe, except that's not the case, because I've been by green trails multiple times.
-Flying sucks ass in the game, you will travel for hundreds kilometers in the DLC areas because the infrastructure is just not there.
-The whole walking around stations and talking to NPCs nonsense, a complete waste of time
-If an NPC starts talking to you, all of your menus are locked until they're finished saying whatever repetitive nonsense they have to say

The game is ass.

it's flat horrible.

If you're looking for a game like this then get X4 Foundations and install the Starwars mod on it and bam...incredible game.

Rebirth tries to thread the needle between X space sim sandbox and something like Dark Star One or Everspace and fails. If you want the arcade stuff, you'll be annoyed; if you want the space sim stuff, guess what, you'll also be annoyed.

Modders have removed or sanded down many of the game's worst issues. Without mods, for example, you have to deal with space highways with 405 levels of traffic, which means dodging freighters or getting tossed back into 'normal' space all while the game's framerate tanks.

Pretty much every campaign mission has had a bug or other issue. At first this was amusing: one of the NPCs instructed me to loot stuff on a space station. Either from the crates or flying over something, I acquired space meth and the station police at the next quest marker discovered it and then blew up my ship lmao 

But eventually I got tired of fighting the game's control, UI, and missions. The story and VA aren't good enough to overcome all the friction.

I wish i could give a maybe. I'm generally a vet of the X games and played them before i had a steam account. this is a fair departure from previous titles. while the player has limitations they nailed the atmosphere. stations feel outwardly alive albeit the npc models are horrendously ugly. I like options this doesn't  give you that in sandbox that feels more of an adventure pilot game. I still however find this title enjoyable for what it's worth.(side note) really wish they brought the station modules from rebirth to X4 lol.

This game gets a lot of hate because it was more limited and not as deep than previous X Games but I really enjoyed it overall.  I wish some of the mechanics like using a mine drone would have carried over to X4.
Objectively the worst X game.  Don't buy this, and let's all collectively forget it ever existed.
